content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 2, 2017

Krishan Chopra
Senior Assistant City Attorney
500 Castro Street
Post Office Box 7540
Mountain View, CA 94039-7540

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-127

Dear Mr. Chopra:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mountain View City
Councilmember Margaret Abe-Koga regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when
rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your
facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Abe-Koga from taking part in governmental
decisions relating to a proposed hotel development project (the "Project") given that her residence
is located within 669 feet of the Project and the that Project will lead to a significant intensification
of use in the immediate area surrounding it?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property interest in her
residence.

FACTS

You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Mountain View and the authorized
representative of Councilmember Abe-Koga.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-127
Page No. 2

The Mountain View City Council will soon consider a developer's proposal for boutique
lifestyle hotel in the City's downtown at the corner of El Camino Real and Castro Street (the
"Project"). The seven-story, 160-room hotel would have a rooftop deck, ground-floor restaurant,
and retail storefronts. The Project would also include a 7,500-square-foot public plaza with seating
for approximately 75 patrons. Project construction would take 12 to 14 months.

The Councilmember owns her residence, a single-story ranch style home located
approximately 669 feet northeast of the closest boundary of the Project, within the City's Old
Mountain View neighborhood. The neighborhood is separated from Castro Street by one block of
commercial parcels which line Castro Street. The Old Mountain View neighborhood is a quiet but
dense residential neighborhood immediately east of City's downtown. The Councilmember's
residence is three residential street blocks north and one street block east of the Project.

The area of the City's downtown that encompasses the Project site is densely developed and
mixed in composition. The Project site is located at one of the busiest intersections in the City: El
Camino Real is a City-wide main thoroughfare; and Castro Street, the primary commercial street
running through the City's downtown, is lined by popular businesses, restaurants, and shops. You
note that it is not uncommon for vehicles to use side streets within the Old Mountain View
neighborhood as "short cuts" when traffic levels are high on El Camino Real and Castro Street.

The Project site covers two contiguous real-property parcels: an 8,374-square-foot privately
owned commercial property, located at 903 Castro Street, and an 11,636-square-foot City-owned
parcel designated as "Gateway Park," located at the northeast corner of El Camino Real and Castro
Street. Currently, the commercial property houses a small bike shop, and Gateway Park consists of
a small, lightly used green area and memorial site. You note that the Project will lead to a
significant intensification of use in the immediate area surrounding it.

Parking is currently at a premium near the Project site and throughout the Old Mountain
View neighborhood. Currently, there are five to seven parking spaces located in front of the bike
shop on Castro Street, and Gateway Park has no parking spaces. Although there are some public
parking garages in the downtown area, there are none in the immediate vicinity of the Project. You
note that it is not uncommon for people to park on residential roads nearby Castro Street to access
businesses, shops, and restaurants.

The hotel would include a one-level subterranean parking structure, measuring 16,000
square feet, with 64 parking stalls or 0.4 stalls per hotel room. The hotel would only provide valet
parking. To make room for the public plaza, the Project would close off a half block of Fairmont
Avenue, severing Fairmont Avenue's existing connection to Castro Street. The portion of Fairmont
Avenue that would be closed off currently has on-street parking on both sides of the street.

Drawing a circle with a 750-foot radius around the Project (slightly greater than the 669 feet
distance the Councilmember's residence is located from the Project), there are 114 real property
parcels within that circle, or approximately 0.5 percent of the 22,799 real property parcels within
the City.

File No. A-17-127
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
Section 87103 sets forth an official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict
of interest under the Act. Of those interests, the decisions at issue and the facts presented implicate
the Councilmember's real-property interest in her residence.

Foreseeability and Materiality

For an interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision,² the decision's effect need not be
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable; in general, if the effect can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an official's real-property interest is material whenever the decision:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy,
noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would
affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has an
interest; or

* * *

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would
influence the market value of the official's property."

Intensity of Use

As a threshold consideration, we note that the City's entire downtown is already very
densely developed, and residents of the Old Mountain View neighborhood are accustomed to living
nearby popular commercial businesses that attract vehicles and pedestrians. This analysis focuses
on potential intensifications of use that may result from the Project.

² An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if it is a named party or the subject of the decision, and an
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Regulation 18701(a).)

File No. A-17-127
Page No. 4

The Project site is currently made up of a small bike shop and a small lightly used public
park. The Project will include a 160-room hotel with a rooftop deck, ground-floor restaurant, retail
shops, and public plaza. These new commercial uses will attract additional patrons, pedestrians, and
vehicles. Furthermore, you note that the Project will lead to a significant intensification of use in the
immediate area surrounding it.

Therefore, we find that the Project would substantially increase the intensity of use of the
properties surrounding the Councilmember's residence.

Traffic Levels & Parking

The Project is located at the corner of El Camino Real and Castro Street, one of the busiest
intersections in the City. The hotel will only provide valet parking, and the valet-parking process
would further bog down traffic at that intersection. You note that it is not uncommon for vehicles to
use side streets within the Old Mountain View neighborhood as "short cuts" when traffic levels are
high on El Camino Real and Castro Street. And the Project would close off half a block of Fairmont
Avenue, where it currently connects with Castro Street, to accommodate the Project's public plaza,
thereby closing off one of the neighborhood's vehicular access points to Castro Street closest to the
Councilmember's residence. These considerations all indicate that the Project will substantially
increase traffic in the immediate area surrounding it.

Parking is at a premium near the Project site and throughout the Old Mountain View
neighborhood due to existing dense commercial and residential development throughout the area.
The 160-room hotel would include a subterranean parking structure with 64 parking stalls, or 0.4
stalls per hotel room, but would not provide sufficient additional parking to meet the extra demand
resultant from the Project. There are five to seven parking spaces in front of the bike shop currently
located at the Project site, but the hotel will only provide valet parking in the subterranean parking
structure. Because the Project would close off the half block of Fairmont Avenue, the on-street
parking on that portion of Fairmont Avenue will be lost. For all these reasons, the Project would
substantially increase the demand for parking nearby.

You note that it is not uncommon for people to park on residential roads nearby Castro
Street to access businesses, shops, and restaurants. Given the additional parking demand created by
the Project, and the lack of additional parking facilities to meet that demand, it is highly likely that
the Project will result in additional parking on the residential streets nearby the Project.

Therefore, we find that the Project would substantially increase the traffic levels, including
the demand for parking, in the portion of the Old Mountain View neighborhood nearby the Project,
including the properties surrounding the Councilmember's residence.

Other Considerations

The Old Mountain View neighborhood is essentially flat, and homeowners do not enjoy
"views" in the same manner as a seaside or mountain community. The Councilmember's residence
is a single-story ranch-style home that is fenced on all sides with an accessory dwelling unit and
mature trees in the backyard. Therefore, the Project is unlikely to affect or alter the view from the
Councilmember's residence.

File No. A-17-127
Page No. 5

The Project would subject the Councilmember's residence to additional noise due to
performances or gatherings at the public plaza or the hotel's rooftop deck, and the general increase
in the intensity of use of the immediate area. And the substantial increase in traffic levels in the
immediate area of the Project will result in increased air pollution. Therefore, the Project would
alter or affect the noise and air quality levels at the Councilmember's residence.

Market Value of the Councilmember's Residence

While you note that the Project will result in an intensification of use in the immediate area
surrounding it, you also point out that countervailing benefits of the Project, such as having the
public plaza and new commercial businesses nearby, may also increase the value of the
Councilmember's residence. However, it is important to keep in mind that the Act prohibits an
official from taking part in a decision if it will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on one or more of the official's interests, regardless of whether the material effect on that
interest is beneficial or detrimental.

The decisions relating to the Project would substantially alter or affect the intensity of use,
traffic levels, and parking in the portion of the Old Mountain View neighborhood nearby the Project
in which the Councilmember's residence is located. Furthermore, the Project would affect the noise
and air quality at, and the privacy of, the Councilmember's residence. Therefore, we find that the
decisions at issue would affect, and would cause a reasonably prudent person to believe those
decisions would influence, the market value of the Councilmember's residence. Accordingly, the
Act would prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they would
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property interest in her residence,
unless that effect is indistinguishable from those decisions effect on the public generally.

Public Generally Exception

Regulation 18703(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant
segment of the public is affected and the effect on the official's interest is not unique compared to
the effect on the significant segment. As is pertinent to the facts presented, Regulation 18703(b)(2)
defines a "significant segment" as at least 25 percent of all residential real property within the
official's jurisdiction.

Drawing a circle with a 750-foot radius around the Project (slightly greater than the 669-foot
distance the Councilmember's residence is located from the Project), there are 114 real property
parcels within that circle, or approximately 0.5 percent of the 22,799 real property parcels within
the City, and some of those parcels would not be residential parcels. Therefore, the Councilmember
has not established that a significant segment of the public will be affected by the decisions at issue.

Thus, the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the
Project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
real-property interest in her residence.

File No. A-17-127
Page No. 6

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFCjgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Abe-Koga from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to a proposed hotel development project (the "Project") given that her residence 
is located within 669 feet of the Project and the that Project will lead to a significant intensification 
of use in the immediate area surrounding it?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property interest in her 
residence.

decisions relating to a proposed hotel development project (the "Project") given that her residence

of use in the immediate area surrounding it?
